item.title: Re: Welcome to Scantron's coldpost theater.
item.content:encoded:
Dearth means "an inadequate supply; scarcity; lack:"

-from dictionary.com

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's interesting, but I think it's a little too subtle. In particular, the penultimate paragraph of the description, the one concerning accountability, could be more clearly worded. On my first and second reads through, I read it that accountability was required for the effects to occur, not the other way around. This seemed to fit with the Incident report—as soon as accountability disappeared, so did effects. However, I don't think this was what you were going for.

I like it, but I would prefer a little less obliqueness. I'm close to an up vote, but I think SCPs that require reading of the comments to come to an understanding are problematic.

item.title: Re: Welcome to Scantron's coldpost theater.
item.content:encoded: Was this a typo? "A request for funding to "investigate the almost certainly anomalous dearth of documentation pertaining to past interrogatees" has been denied."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you may not understand what's happening here…those red links at the bottom are collapsibles that contain further information.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Odd. Potential to be interesting, but right now it is just a room with broken hardware.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy fuck. I had to drop by here and reread, because there's a subtle bit I completely glossed over the first time through (namely, the first/second test difference; I thought they were the same) that distracted me. +1 I do love me some conservation of detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't notice that upon my first read or my second read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, the physical alterations persist after leaving the room?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a pretty good interpretation of the material. There's no substantial alternative understanding that I was hoping for people to come to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So re-reading it I think I (kind) of get it.

Spoilers?
– hide block

So this is an interrogation room where the interrogator can do anything, without consequences (since at the end both persons return to the original state), as long as there is no accountability (this must be the point of the different sentence between test A-8 and A-9). But how reliable is the description? Since the anomalous properties only manifest with two people, and the only way to know what happened is with those people's testimonies, there's actually basically no information detailing what happened; and if the exposure to 2122-1-D results in mental alterations, it can also very well alter the interrogatee so as to not reveal it either. And I guess incident 7 shows that the physical alterations make them super-strong or something similar.
Hm, exposure to 2122-1-D makes for extremely strong and obedient people… I guess the implication of attachment S9 is that the Foundation is making some super soldiers? 2122 would not only (or maybe even mainly) be an interrogation chamber but an indoctrination one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My headcanon is that 'Object Class' and 'Anomaly Class' are interchangeable, depending on which site is writing the documentation.

I see no problem with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's an equal chance that you've missed something, you were supposed to infer it, or I did a bad job making it inferrable.

The bit with SCP-2122-2 is deliberate. The idea is that 2122-2 is whatever happens when you expose someone to 2122-1-D repeatedly, what with the unspecified mental and physical alterations.

I'm glad you enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The pacing and tone filled me with that marvelous, old-school SCP dread. I was confused on a couple of points, and can't tell exactly if I've missed something, or I'm supposed to be inferring more.

Did you mean to refer to SCP-2122-2 in the final attachment? There's no other mention of that designation, but I can't begin to guess what it might be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Read those test logs more carefully. There is a one-sentence difference. Look over the conditions that affect the anomaly.

These test logs do not serve the same narrative purpose that test logs in most SCPs do. The point is not to show off the nature of the anomaly, but to show how the Foundation thinks to use it. The lines are there purely for you to read between.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The final attachment doesn't seem all that necessary, but otherwise I feel like I see what you were going for, and this is effective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I fail to see the point of the test logs. They're too damn vague and don't give interesting examples, and I fail to see how the first two tests differed in methodology in any way; to me, it looks like the same test done twice, no differences. If the exact same test was performed under the exact same conditions, test B shouldn't give a drastically different result to test A.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea behind it is that "Anomaly Class" can be used for every SCP and be appropriate, whether or not it's an object, thus making it a more logical word to stick near the top of every article.

The format screw is very rarely necessary, but it's definitely justifiable, and that's good enough for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The format screw doesn't seem necessary though. It's a room, which is an object, so Object Class works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dude, it's Scantron.

He loves to fuck with the Object Class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, it does that, but if that's the main thing you got out of this, then one of us is fucking up horribly.

Anyone who is unsure what I'm getting at here should really refer back to that Foucault quote I made in the first post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So basically, this is taking the "torture always works" trope and SCPifying it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Figure it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anomaly Class

Wait what?

item.title: Welcome to Scantron's coldpost theater.
item.content:encoded:
+ Inspiration
- Inspiration

"Discipline is essentially centripetal. I mean that discipline functions to the extent that it isolates a space, that it determines a segment. Discipline concentrates, focuses, and encloses. The first action of discipline is in fact to circumscribe a space in which its power and the mechanisms of its power will function fully and without limit…"
- Michel Foucault, Security, Territory, Population
